title: Drager Fabius Anesthesia Machine - Ventilator Fails Self-Test / Won’t Start
description: Guide for troubleshooting a Fabius anesthesia machine ventilator that fails self-test or won't start, focusing on external causes and patient safety.

steps.title: Ensure Power Supply and Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is plugged into a verified hospital-grade outlet. Check that the main power switch is ON. Inspect the power cord for damage. Why it matters: Anesthesia machines will not complete self-tests without stable power.

steps.title: Check Backup Battery Status
steps.instructions: If the machine uses a battery, verify it is charged and properly seated. Some Fabius models require a battery to be functional to pass self-test.

steps.title: Verify Gas Supply and Hoses
steps.instructions: Ensure all medical gases (O₂, N₂O, air) are connected and pressure is within specification. Confirm that the hoses are properly attached and there are no leaks. Why it matters: Gas pressure sensors can prevent self-test completion if insufficient.

steps.title: Inspect Breathing Circuit and Accessories
steps.instructions: Remove any patient circuit attachments, filters, or breathing bags that may obstruct the system. Ensure that the ventilator circuit is properly mounted and clamps are released. Why it matters: Obstructions or misaligned components can cause self-test failure.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or Fault Indicators
steps.instructions: Review the display for messages that may indicate sensor errors, disconnected components, or service reminders.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Machine
steps.instructions: Turn off the machine, wait 30–60 seconds, and restart. Why it matters: A simple reset often clears temporary faults in software or sensors.

steps.title: Run Self-Test with Minimal Configuration
steps.instructions: Disconnect optional modules or accessories. Run self-test again to see if the issue persists.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator fails self-test and will not start; machine displays error code during pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: Power supply and gas connections verified; breathing circuit removed; self-test continues to fail, suggesting internal ventilator or sensor fault.
CCR.Resolution: Machine removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for Drager bench evaluation/service.

helpfulDetails: Machine status: Out of Service pending repair.

title: Drager Fabius Anesthesia Machine – Battery / UPS Problems
description: Troubleshooting guide for battery faults, charging issues, and UPS transition problems on the Drager Fabius anesthesia machine.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the machine is supporting an active patient unless absolutely necessary. If there is a battery or power failure alarm during a case, ensure the patient is safely transitioned to backup ventilation and stable before proceeding.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the power cord is fully seated in both the rear of the Fabius and the wall outlet. Check for a secure hospital-grade plug connection. Look for any damage to the power cord (cuts, bent prongs, loose strain relief). Test the outlet with a known-good device or outlet tester. A poor AC supply prevents the internal battery from charging and may trigger battery alarms.

steps.title: Check Power Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: Verify the machine indicates AC power present. Confirm no facility power interruptions occurred (tripped breaker, switched outlet, power strip). If connected to an external UPS, verify the UPS is powered on and not in fault mode.

steps.title: Simulate Power Loss (When Safe and Off Patient)
steps.instructions: With the unit powered on and not in clinical use, disconnect AC power. Confirm the unit transitions to battery power without shutting down. Observe for immediate low battery alarms or shutdown. If the unit shuts down immediately, suspect battery degradation or internal power supply issues.

steps.title: Check Battery Status Indicators
steps.instructions: Review battery charge percentage on the display. Note whether the battery appears to charge when AC power is restored. If battery percentage remains static or drops quickly, battery capacity is likely diminished.

steps.title: Consider Battery Age
steps.instructions: Review service records for battery replacement history. Internal batteries in anesthesia machines commonly degrade over 2–4 years depending on usage and environment. If beyond expected lifespan, battery replacement is appropriate.

steps.title: Inspect for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Check for excessive heat around the machine. Listen for abnormal fan noise. Note any burnt smell or unusual heat near the power supply area. These may indicate internal power supply or charging circuit concerns.

steps.title: Check External UPS (If Used)
steps.instructions: Confirm external UPS runtime is adequate. Perform a controlled UPS self-test. Replace UPS batteries if they fail load testing. Ensure the issue is not external before attributing failure to the anesthesia machine.

CCR.Complaint: Anesthesia machine alarming for low battery and shuts down when unplugged.
CCR.Cause: Battery failed load test. Unit powered off immediately when AC disconnected. Battery age 4+ years.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced internal battery pack. Verified proper charging and confirmed successful transition to battery power during simulated power loss test.

title: Drager Perseus Ventilator / Bellows Not Cycling
description: Guide for troubleshooting situations where the Perseus anesthesia machine bellows do not cycle or ventilator fails to deliver breaths.

steps.title: Confirm Machine Power
steps.instructions: Verify that the anesthesia machine is plugged in and switched on. Check that battery backup (if present) is charged and functional. Reason: Without proper power, bellows cannot cycle.

steps.title: Verify Gas Supply
steps.instructions: Check that oxygen, air, and nitrous oxide supply pressures are within normal limits. Ensure the gas cylinders are open or pipeline supply is active. Reason: Ventilation is driven by gas flow; low or absent pressure prevents cycling.

steps.title: Inspect Breathing Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the inspiratory and expiratory limbs are correctly connected. Confirm that the patient Y-piece is attached properly. Look for kinks, disconnections, or leaks. Reason: Bellows will not rise if the circuit is blocked or has leaks.

steps.title: Check the Ventilator Mode and Settings
steps.instructions: Verify the ventilator mode is correctly selected (e.g., Volume Control, Pressure Control). Confirm tidal volume, respiratory rate, and pressure limits are set appropriately. Reason: Improper settings can prevent cycling or trigger safety stops.

steps.title: Assess Bellows Assembly
steps.instructions: Confirm that the bellows are seated correctly on the mount. Check that the bellows are not stuck, collapsed, or obstructed. Reason: Physical misalignment or obstruction can prevent movement.

steps.title: Examine Safety Valves
steps.instructions: Inspect APL (Adjustable Pressure-Limiting) valve and ventilator relief valves to ensure they are in correct position. Reason: Incorrect valve positions can prevent proper bellows cycling.

steps.title: Perform Leak Test / System Check
steps.instructions: Run the anesthesia machine’s automated system check per Drager prompts. Observe any alarms or error messages. Reason: Detects issues in ventilator drive system or circuit integrity.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator bellows not cycling during routine use.
CCR.Cause: External power, gas supply, and circuit connections verified; bellows seated properly; ventilator mode and safety valves checked. Issue persists, likely internal ventilator drive failure.
CCR.Resolution: Machine removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for manufacturer-authorized repair.

helpfulDetails: Circuit inspected, no kinks or leaks

title: Drager Perseus Anesthesia Machine - Gas Delivery / FiO₂ Mismatch
description: Troubleshooting guide for FiO₂ mismatch and incorrect gas delivery on the Drager Perseus anesthesia machine.

steps.title: Verify Oxygen and Air Supply
steps.instructions: Ensure both the oxygen and compressed air sources are connected and turned on. Check that pipeline pressures are within the normal operating range (usually 50–60 psi).

steps.title: Inspect Supply Hoses and Connections
steps.instructions: Look for kinks, leaks, or disconnections in oxygen and air hoses. Ensure quick-connect fittings are fully seated.

steps.title: Check Flowmeters and Vaporizers
steps.instructions: Confirm that flowmeter readings correspond to set values. Ensure vaporizers are properly seated and turned off if not in use.

steps.title: Examine the Oxygen Sensor
steps.instructions: Verify the FiO₂ sensor is connected, powered, and calibrated per manufacturer guidance. Look for condensation, debris, or damage on the sensor.

steps.title: Run Self-Test / System Check
steps.instructions: Perform the machine’s automated pre-use check. Note any warnings or errors related to oxygen supply, low FiO₂, or sensor malfunction.

steps.title: Verify Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm FiO₂, tidal volume, and fresh gas flow settings match the intended prescription. Ensure no cross-coupled settings or unintended gas blender override.

CCR.Complaint: Anesthesia machine FiO₂ shows 40% but set at 60%.
CCR.Cause: Observed sensor connected but not calibrated; supply pressures slightly low; hoses intact.
CCR.Resolution: Verified supply, re-ran self-test, calibrated sensor; FiO₂ matched set value. If unresolved, machine removed from service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Supply pressures for O₂ and air
FiO₂ sensor status and last calibration date

title: Drager Perseus Anesthesia Machine - Startup / Self-Test Failures
description: Guide for troubleshooting Drager Perseus anesthesia machine startup and self-test failures, emphasizing external checks before internal escalation.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the machine is plugged into a properly rated, live outlet. Check for tripped circuit breakers or unplugged power cords. Reason: Insufficient or unstable power can prevent self-test completion.

steps.title: Check Battery / UPS Status
steps.instructions: Confirm internal battery or UPS (if installed) is charged and connected. Reason: Low or missing battery can trigger startup failures even on mains power.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections
steps.instructions: Detach and reattach all external modules, monitors, and gas supplies. Ensure gas cylinders are opened or piped gases are online. Reason: The machine may fail self-test if it detects missing critical modules or gas supply.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages or Codes
steps.instructions: Document any displayed error codes. Cross-reference with Drager troubleshooting charts (without opening the unit). Reason: Codes can indicate specific subsystems preventing startup.

steps.title: Restart Procedure
steps.instructions: Power down the machine, wait 30 seconds, then power back on. Reason: Temporary software or sensor glitches can cause self-test failures.

steps.title: Environmental Check
steps.instructions: Ensure ambient temperature and humidity are within operating limits. Reason: Extreme environmental conditions can cause startup errors.

CCR.Complaint: Machine fails startup self-test and displays error code 105.
CCR.Cause: Power and external connections verified; error persists on multiple restart attempts.
CCR.Resolution: Machine removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to vendor for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested with multimeter or known-working device
Circuit breakers and power strips checked

title: Drager Fabius Alarm Failures / False Alarms
description: Guide for troubleshooting false alarms or alarm failures on Drager Fabius anesthesia machines.

steps.title: Verify Machine Power and Backup
steps.instructions: Ensure the machine is plugged into a verified working outlet and powered on. Check battery status if operating on internal battery.

steps.title: Inspect Alarm and Sensor Connections
steps.instructions: Verify that all breathing circuits, O₂ sensors, pressure lines, and flow sensors are properly connected. Check that connectors are clean, dry, and free of damage.

steps.title: Check Gas Supply and Flow
steps.instructions: Confirm that O₂, N₂O, and air supply lines are connected and pressure is within normal operating range. Verify that flowmeters are not blocked or leaking.

steps.title: Examine Breathing Circuit Setup
steps.instructions: Ensure correct circuit type (adult, pediatric) is selected in the machine settings. Check for leaks or obstructions in the circuit and endotracheal tube connections.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Configuration and Limits
steps.instructions: Review the alarm limits set in the machine for O₂, CO₂, airway pressure, tidal volume, etc. Ensure that alarm mute buttons are not inadvertently engaged.

steps.title: Perform a Self-Test / System Check
steps.instructions: Run the machine’s built-in self-test (Drager Fabius includes an automated check). Observe any errors or warnings reported.

steps.title: Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the machine is not exposed to extreme temperatures, moisture, or electrical interference. Remove any nearby equipment that could interfere with sensors.

CCR.Complaint: User reports frequent false alarms and occasional alarm failures on the Fabius machine.
CCR.Cause: Upon inspection, some sensor connectors were loose, and alarm limits were improperly set.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected sensors securely, verified all connections, reset alarm limits, ran self-test, and confirmed alarms functioning correctly.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds or smells: Describe

title: Drager Fabius Touchscreen / Display Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for Drager Fabius anesthesia machine touchscreen and display issues, focusing on external causes before internal component failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is not actively ventilating a patient before troubleshooting. If in use, transition the patient to another anesthesia machine or alternate ventilation source first. Display troubleshooting should never compromise patient ventilation.

steps.title: Verify AC Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is plugged directly into a known-good outlet. Check for tripped breakers or recently used red emergency outlets that may have unstable supply. Review battery status on the screen (if visible). Low voltage or unstable power can cause screen freezes, flickering, or boot issues. Verify outlet tested, power cord secure at both ends, no extension cords, and no power strips.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Reboot
steps.instructions: If safe and the machine is not in use, power down the machine using proper shutdown procedure. Wait 30–60 seconds. Restart the system and observe boot behavior. Observe whether the screen initializes normally, startup tones are normal, and whether the display fails before or after full boot. If the screen restores after reboot, the issue may have been a software hang.

steps.title: Inspect for External Physical Causes
steps.instructions: Check for visible screen cracks or fluid intrusion. Look for condensation or OR cleaning residue near bezel edges. Confirm no adhesive covers or protective films are interfering with touch response. Touchscreens can become unresponsive due to fluid intrusion or contamination.

steps.title: Check for Peripheral Lockups
steps.instructions: If the screen appears frozen but the machine is powered, observe whether alarms are functioning. Check if knobs or physical buttons respond. Disconnect any non-essential external connections (network, USB if applicable). If physical controls work but touchscreen does not, the issue may be isolated to the display module.

steps.title: Evaluate Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Determine if the OR experienced temperature extremes, if the machine was recently moved from cold storage, or if there is visible heat buildup or fan obstruction. Overheating or condensation can cause temporary display malfunction.

steps.title: Observe for Backlight vs. Display Failure
steps.instructions: If the screen appears blank, shine a flashlight at the screen. If faint images are visible, possible backlight issue. If completely dark with no image, possible display power or control issue. This helps differentiate between backlight failure and total display loss.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen not responding to touch in OR 3. Screen frozen during case setup.
CCR.Cause: Unit powered but touchscreen unresponsive. Reboot temporarily restored function. Issue recurred after 15 minutes. No external contamination noted.
CCR.Resolution: Removed anesthesia machine from service. Labeled Out of Service. Scheduled vendor repair for suspected display module failure.

title: Drager Fabius Anesthesia Machine - Low Oxygen Pressure / O₂ Flush Issues
description: Guide for troubleshooting low oxygen pressure or O₂ flush issues on a Drager Fabius anesthesia machine.

steps.title: Verify Oxygen Supply
steps.instructions: Check that the oxygen cylinder or pipeline supply is full and turned on. Confirm the correct pressure at the machine inlet using the pressure gauge. Why it matters: Low or empty supply is the most common cause of low oxygen pressure alarms.

steps.title: Inspect Hoses and Connections
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the oxygen hose from the source to the machine. Ensure all connections are tight and free of kinks, cracks, or leaks. Why it matters: Even small leaks can reduce pressure and prevent the O₂ flush from functioning properly.

steps.title: Check the Oxygen Flowmeter
steps.instructions: Verify that the flowmeter knob is in the correct position. Ensure the flowmeter tube and bobbin move freely. Why it matters: Obstructions or misalignment can prevent proper flow and pressure.

steps.title: Test O₂ Flush Function
steps.instructions: Press the O₂ flush button briefly. Observe whether the oxygen flows rapidly into the circuit. If the flush is weak or absent, note any unusual sounds or behavior. Why it matters: A faulty flush valve or blocked pathway may indicate internal components need servicing.

steps.title: Check for Machine Alarms or Messages
steps.instructions: Review the display panel for any error messages related to oxygen supply or pressure. Follow any suggested external corrective actions listed by the machine.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Machine
steps.instructions: Turn the machine off and back on per manufacturer instructions. Confirm if the low oxygen pressure alarm persists. Why it matters: Some sensors or valves may reset after power cycling.

CCR.Complaint: Low oxygen pressure alarm and weak O₂ flush observed.
CCR.Cause: Oxygen cylinder was low and supply hose had a minor kink reducing flow.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced cylinder, straightened hose, verified O₂ flush function; machine returned to service.

title: Drager Isolette C2000 Infant Incubator – Touch Panel / Control Knob Not Responding
description: Troubleshooting guide for unresponsive touch panel or control knob issues on the Drager Isolette C2000 infant incubator, focusing on external checks before internal repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure the Infant Is Safe Before Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Confirm the infant is stable. If active patient care is involved, transfer the infant to another functioning incubator before performing troubleshooting. Never power cycle during active neonatal support. Patient safety always comes first.

steps.title: Verify Power Source and Power Quality
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is plugged into a tested hospital-grade outlet. Check that the outlet is functioning (use an outlet tester if needed). Verify the AC power indicator is illuminated. Check for recent power interruptions or brownouts reported by staff.

steps.title: Check for Panel Lock or Keypad Lock Function
steps.instructions: Confirm the control panel is not in “Lock” or “Standby” mode. Attempt to unlock per normal user interface steps. Verify no lock indicator icons are displayed.

steps.title: Inspect the Touch Panel Surface
steps.instructions: Look for residual cleaning solution, moisture, cracked overlay, delamination, or sticky residue. Gently clean the surface with approved disinfectant and dry thoroughly.

steps.title: Assess Control Knob Function
steps.instructions: Rotate the knob slowly and observe if values change. Press inward (if push-to-select style) and observe for feedback. Check for mechanical binding, uneven rotation, or excessive looseness.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Cycle (If Safe)
steps.instructions: Only perform this step if the infant has been transferred and no therapy is actively dependent on this incubator. Turn the unit off using the normal shutdown process. Wait 60–90 seconds. Restore power. Observe whether the screen boots normally, touch response returns, or any error codes appear.

steps.title: Observe for Additional Symptoms
steps.instructions: Document any delayed screen refresh, random beeping, display flicker, alarm messages, or burning smell or abnormal heat near control panel.

CCR.Complaint: NICU staff reported the incubator control panel was not responding to touch, and temperature settings could not be adjusted.
CCR.Cause: Touch panel remained unresponsive after cleaning and power cycle. Control knob rotation did not change settings. No external power issues found. Likely internal UI interface failure.
CCR.Resolution: Unit removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to shop for further evaluation. Replacement incubator provided to unit.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (Removed from service / Operational after reboot)

title: Drager Isolette C2000 Infant Incubator – Temperature Control Errors (Too Hot / Too Cold)
description: Troubleshooting guide for Drager Isolette C2000 temperature control errors, including overheating, underheating, and failure to maintain setpoint.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the incubator is actively being used, transfer the infant to another verified working incubator before troubleshooting. Do not attempt troubleshooting while supporting a patient inside the unit. Why: Temperature instability can directly impact neonatal safety.

steps.title: Verify Mode of Operation (Air Mode vs. Skin Mode)
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the incubator is operating in Air Temperature Mode or Skin (Servo) Mode. Verify the correct setpoint for the selected mode. Why: Incorrect mode selection can create perceived overheating or underheating.

steps.title: Check Skin Probe (If in Servo Mode)
steps.instructions: Ensure the skin probe is securely connected to the incubator. Inspect the probe cable for damage. Confirm proper probe placement on the infant (abdomen, secure adhesion). If available, test with a known good probe. Why: A disconnected or faulty probe can cause runaway heating or false low-temperature readings.

steps.title: Inspect Air Temperature Sensor Area
steps.instructions: Check that the internal air sensor is not obstructed. Look for buildup of lint, debris, or residue. Why: Obstructed airflow around the sensor can cause inaccurate readings.

steps.title: Check Doors, Access Ports, and Seals
steps.instructions: Ensure all portholes and access doors are fully closed. Inspect door gaskets for gaps or damage. Why: Air leaks commonly cause low-temperature alarms or inability to maintain setpoint.

steps.title: Inspect Air Circulation
steps.instructions: Listen for the circulation fan operating normally. Check for unusual noise or lack of airflow. Ensure nothing is blocking internal air vents. Why: Poor circulation leads to temperature stratification and control instability.

steps.title: Evaluate Heater Function
steps.instructions: In a safe, empty unit, monitor temperature rise from ambient to setpoint. Confirm steady, gradual temperature increase. Watch for overshoot beyond setpoint. Why: Overshoot may indicate sensor calibration issues or control instability. Failure to heat suggests heater or power delivery problems.

steps.title: Verify Calibration Status
steps.instructions: Review last preventive maintenance date. If temperature deviation is suspected, compare displayed temperature with a calibrated external thermometer during controlled testing. Why: Sensor drift over time can cause inaccurate control.

steps.title: Check for Error Codes or Alarm Messages
steps.instructions: Document exact wording of alarms. Clear alarms and observe recurrence pattern. Why: Repeating alarms may indicate internal sensor or control board faults.

CCR.Complaint: Incubator alarming for high temperature and not maintaining setpoint in servo mode.
CCR.Cause: Skin probe cable intermittently open; unit defaulted to uncontrolled heating response.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced skin probe with known good probe, verified stable temperature control in servo mode. Unit returned to service.

title: Drager Isolette C2000 Infant Incubator – Humidity Not Working or Leaking
description: Troubleshooting guide for Drager Isolette C2000 humidity system issues including low humidity, no humidity, leaks, and humidity-related alarms.

steps.title: Ensure the Infant Is Safely Relocated
steps.instructions: Transfer the patient to another properly functioning incubator. Do not troubleshoot humidity issues with an active patient inside. Humidity instability can impact thermoregulation and infection risk in neonates.

steps.title: Confirm Humidity Mode Is Enabled
steps.instructions: Verify humidity is turned ON in the control settings. Verify a target humidity level is set. Verify the system is not in a mode that disables humidification. Humidity heaters will not energize if humidity is disabled in software.

steps.title: Check Water Reservoir Level
steps.instructions: Remove and inspect the water reservoir. Confirm it contains sterile or distilled water (per facility policy). Ensure it is not empty. Ensure it is not overfilled past the maximum fill line. Observe for cracks, discoloration, warping, or mineral buildup. If empty, refill and properly reseat.

steps.title: Verify Reservoir Seating and Alignment
steps.instructions: Confirm it clicks fully into place. Confirm it sits flush with the housing. Confirm no gaps are visible. Confirm the latch (if applicable) is secure. Improper seating can prevent water from feeding to the heater, trigger false low humidity alarms, and cause external leaking.

steps.title: Inspect for External Leaks
steps.instructions: Check around the reservoir gasket or seal, bottom of the humidity compartment, under the unit base, and around drain ports if applicable. Look for water pooling, damp surfaces, or mineral residue trails. If leaking is present, remove the reservoir, inspect gasket for cracks, flattening, or debris, and clean mating surfaces.

steps.title: Check for Obstruction or Mineral Buildup
steps.instructions: Inspect the humidity chamber area without deep disassembly. Look for visible scale buildup and confirm the water pathway is not blocked. Mineral buildup can prevent proper vapor generation or water flow. If heavy buildup is suspected, remove from service for cleaning per facility procedure.

steps.title: Monitor Heater Activation Indicators
steps.instructions: With humidity enabled and reservoir properly installed, listen for normal heater cycling sounds. Monitor humidity percentage increase over 10–15 minutes. Check for error codes. If humidity does not increase at all, the heater does not appear to energize, or alarms persist, the issue may involve the humidity heater element, temperature sensor, or control board.

steps.title: Check Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Confirm room temperature is within normal operating range. Confirm access doors and portholes are fully closed. Confirm gaskets around doors are intact. Open panels or poor sealing can cause humidity loss that mimics system failure.

CCR.Complaint: NICU staff reported humidity not increasing above 30% and water leaking from base of incubator.
CCR.Cause: Water reservoir improperly seated; gasket folded under lip causing leak and poor water feed to heater.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated reservoir properly, corrected gasket alignment, verified humidity increased to setpoint (60%) within 15 minutes. No further leakage observed. Returned unit to service.

title: Drager Babylog VN500 Ventilator Circuit Leak / High Leak Alarm Troubleshooting
description: Guide to troubleshoot high leak or circuit leak alarms on Drager Babylog VN500 ventilators, focusing on external causes first.

steps.title: Check Patient Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure all tubing, endotracheal or tracheostomy connections are secure. Inspect for loose or disconnected connectors at the Y-piece and patient interface. Why: Even a small disconnection can trigger a high leak alarm.

steps.title: Inspect Circuit for Damage
steps.instructions: Examine inspiratory and expiratory tubing for cracks, holes, or tears. Replace any damaged tubing immediately. Why: Damaged circuits compromise pressure delivery and tidal volume.

steps.title: Verify Humidifier and Water Trap
steps.instructions: Ensure the humidifier chamber and water trap are correctly seated. Check for condensation buildup causing unintended leaks. Why: Condensation can create low points or gaps, simulating leaks.

steps.title: Check Filters and Connectors
steps.instructions: Confirm bacterial/viral filters are correctly installed and intact. Inspect for tears, cracks, or loose fittings. Why: Faulty filters or connectors can be a source of leaks.

steps.title: Perform Circuit Leak Test
steps.instructions: Use the ventilator’s built-in leak test function. Compare measured leak with expected thresholds for the patient setup. Why: Confirms whether the leak is in the circuit versus ventilator internal components.

steps.title: Reposition or Replace Circuit Components
steps.instructions: If leak persists, systematically replace or reposition tubing, connectors, or humidifier chamber. Why: Stepwise replacement isolates the exact location of the leak.

CCR.Complaint: Patient ventilator alarms “High Leak” during therapy.
CCR.Cause: External circuit inspection revealed loose Y-piece connector and minor condensation buildup in the humidifier.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected Y-piece, drained condensation, and verified leak within acceptable range via built-in leak test.

helpfulDetails: Outlet and gas supply pressure verified

title: Drager Babylog VN500 Ventilator - Ventilator Won’t Deliver Breath / No Output
description: Guide for troubleshooting a Babylog VN500 ventilator that fails to deliver breaths or shows no output, focusing on external causes first.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the ventilator is connected to a live outlet or has a fully charged battery. Check the main power switch is ON. Why: No power or low battery will prevent breath delivery.

steps.title: Inspect Circuit Connections
steps.instructions: Check all patient circuit tubing for proper attachment to the ventilator ports. Ensure the inspiratory and expiratory limbs are connected correctly. Why: A disconnected or reversed circuit prevents the ventilator from delivering breaths.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions or Kinks
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the breathing circuit, filters, and tubing for kinks, occlusions, or moisture buildup. Remove any visible blockage. Why: Obstructions can trigger a no-output condition or alarm.

steps.title: Review Alarm Messages and Settings
steps.instructions: Check the ventilator display for active alarms (e.g., low pressure, high PEEP, circuit disconnection). Confirm the ventilator mode, tidal volume, and respiratory rate are correctly set. Why: Improper settings or active alarms can inhibit breath delivery.

steps.title: Circuit Leak Test
steps.instructions: Perform the ventilator’s self-test or leak check if prompted. Observe for leak indications and correct as needed. Why: Significant leaks in the circuit prevent effective ventilation.

steps.title: Confirm Gas Supply
steps.instructions: Ensure medical air and oxygen sources are available and pressure is within the acceptable range. Verify that the ventilator is properly switched to the correct gas source. Why: No or insufficient gas supply prevents breath generation.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator is not delivering breaths to the patient.
CCR.Cause: No power issue, circuit connections verified, settings and gas supply checked; device still shows no output.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for bench evaluation/repair.

title: Drager Babylog VN500 Ventilator Touchscreen / Control Panel Issues
description: Guide for troubleshooting unresponsive or erratic touchscreen/control panel on the Babylog VN500 ventilator, focusing on external causes before escalation.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Check that the ventilator is properly connected to a reliable power outlet. Confirm that the main power switch is on and that the display is receiving power. Why: A low or intermittent power source can cause the touchscreen to behave erratically.

steps.title: Check Battery Backup (if applicable)
steps.instructions: If running on battery, ensure it is fully charged and properly seated. Switch to AC power to see if the touchscreen responds. Why: Weak batteries can reduce touchscreen responsiveness or disable the panel.

steps.title: Inspect Connections and Cables
steps.instructions: Examine the power cable and any data/control connections for damage, loose connections, or kinks. Reseat or replace cables if needed. Why: Loose or damaged connections can interrupt panel functionality.

steps.title: Restart the Device
steps.instructions: Perform a proper ventilator shutdown and restart according to Drager guidelines. Observe if the touchscreen initializes correctly after reboot. Why: A system reset can resolve software glitches causing panel issues.

steps.title: Clean the Touchscreen Surface
steps.instructions: Use a soft, lint-free cloth slightly dampened with approved cleaning solution. Avoid excessive moisture or harsh chemicals. Why: Debris, fingerprints, or fluids on the screen can prevent accurate touch detection.

steps.title: Check for Error Messages / Panel Lock
steps.instructions: Look for any messages indicating a touchscreen or panel lock mode. Release lock or follow on-screen instructions if applicable.

steps.title: Verify Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is not exposed to extreme temperatures, moisture, or strong electromagnetic interference. Why: Environmental factors can affect touchscreen sensitivity or electronic circuits.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator touchscreen not responding during routine use.
CCR.Cause: Power and connection verified; screen remains unresponsive despite reset.
CCR.Resolution: Ventilator removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for manufacturer repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status documented as Out of Service

title: Drager Evita Infinity V500 – Inspiratory/Expiratory Flow Sensor Contamination or Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting flow sensor contamination or failure on the Drager Evita Infinity V500 ventilator, focusing on external checks first.

steps.title: Verify Power and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is plugged into a stable power source. Check that all cables and connectors for flow sensors and the patient circuit are securely attached. Reason: Loose or intermittent connections can mimic sensor failure.

steps.title: Inspect Patient Circuit
steps.instructions: Remove and examine the inspiratory and expiratory tubing for visible moisture, condensation, or obstruction. Ensure that filters, humidifiers, and condensate traps are properly seated and not blocked. Reason: Contaminated or obstructed circuits can trigger false flow sensor alarms.

steps.title: Check Sensor Orientation and Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the flow sensor is installed in the correct orientation as per labeling (inspiratory vs expiratory). Confirm the sensor is fully seated and locked in place. Reason: Misalignment can cause inaccurate readings or contamination warnings.

steps.title: Clean or Replace Disposable Flow Sensors
steps.instructions: If using disposable or single-patient sensors, replace with a new unit. If reusable, follow manufacturer-approved cleaning and drying procedures. Reason: Contamination buildup affects sensor accuracy.

steps.title: Run Sensor Calibration/Auto-Zero Procedure
steps.instructions: Navigate to the ventilator’s service or pre-use check menu and perform flow sensor calibration. Observe whether calibration completes successfully or produces error messages. Reason: Ensures sensor electronics are reading correctly after cleaning or replacement.

steps.title: Observe for Alarms or Unusual Behavior
steps.instructions: Monitor tidal volume, inspiratory, and expiratory flow readings during a self-test or with a test lung. Listen for abnormal airflow sounds or pressure fluctuations. Reason: Confirms functional correction before returning device to patient use.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displayed expiratory flow sensor failure during pre-use check; tidal volumes appear inaccurate.
CCR.Cause: Inspection revealed moisture accumulation in the expiratory flow sensor and tubing; calibration failed before cleaning.
CCR.Resolution: Flow sensor and circuit replaced per protocol, sensor cleaned/replaced, calibration successfully completed, ventilator returned to service with no alarms.

helpfulDetails: Check if patient circuit contains condensation or water
Note sensor serial number and type
Record final ventilator status after troubleshooting

title: Drager Evita Infinity V500 – Internal Turbine or Blower Performance Degradation
description: Guide for troubleshooting reduced turbine or blower performance on the Drager Evita Infinity V500 ventilator, focusing on external causes before internal repair.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm ventilator is plugged into a functional wall outlet or fully charged battery. Check power cords and connectors for visible damage. Why: Inconsistent voltage can cause blower performance warnings.

steps.title: Check Airway Circuit and Filters
steps.instructions: Inspect inspiratory and expiratory circuits for kinks, occlusions, or condensation. Remove and inspect bacterial/HEPA filters; replace if clogged. Why: Restricted airflow can mimic turbine degradation.

steps.title: Confirm Humidifier and Heated Circuit Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure that any humidifier water chamber is not overfilled or misaligned. Heated circuits should be intact and connected correctly. Why: Blocked or misaligned humidifiers increase airway resistance.

steps.title: Run Internal Self-Test / Pre-Use Check
steps.instructions: Use the ventilator’s built-in diagnostic menu to perform a turbine self-test. Observe for error codes, slow spin-up, or abnormal alarm patterns. Why: Confirms whether the blower is internally degraded or a circuit issue.

steps.title: Check Exhaust and Cooling Paths
steps.instructions: Inspect vents and fan pathways for dust, lint, or obstruction. Clean any accessible surfaces following infection control protocols. Why: Poor ventilation can reduce turbine efficiency.

steps.title: Evaluate Alarm and Error Logs
steps.instructions: Review logged events in the ventilator menu for turbine-specific alarms. Note any repeated performance warnings or airflow errors. Why: Patterns can indicate intermittent blower degradation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinician reports ventilator unable to reach set tidal volumes; turbine alarm displayed intermittently.
CCR.Cause: External inspection shows airway circuits and filters clear; pre-use self-test indicates turbine performance degradation.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for manufacturer-authorized repair.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual turbine sounds or airflow delay
Final device status and out-of-service labeling

title: Drager Evita Infinity V500 – Battery Module Failure or Charging Circuit Faults
description: Troubleshoot battery failures and charging circuit faults on the Drager Evita Infinity V500 ventilator, focusing on external causes before internal repair.

steps.title: Confirm AC Power Availability
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is plugged into a known working outlet. Verify outlet voltage with a portable meter if available. Why: Battery will not charge if AC power is absent or unstable.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Module
steps.instructions: Remove the battery and visually inspect for damage, corrosion, or debris on contacts. Re-seat the battery firmly, ensuring proper alignment. Why: Poor connection can prevent charging or cause false failure messages.

steps.title: Check Battery Module Indicators
steps.instructions: Observe any LEDs or status indicators on the battery module. Compare with normal behavior documented in the device’s quick reference. Why: Indicators often reveal if the module is recognized or in error state.

steps.title: Swap with Known-Good Battery
steps.instructions: If available, replace with another V500 battery module. Observe if charging and runtime normalize. Why: Confirms whether issue is battery-specific or internal to the ventilator.

steps.title: Check Ventilator Charging Circuit Status
steps.instructions: Use the ventilator’s self-test or system information menu to confirm battery charging function. Why: The device may report charging faults separately from the battery.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Connectors
steps.instructions: Check the battery connector and ventilator port for dirt, bent pins, or wear. Clean lightly with approved materials if needed. Why: Electrical contact issues are a common cause of charging failure.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Ventilator
steps.instructions: Turn off the ventilator, wait 30 seconds, then power on. Recheck battery charging behavior. Why: Can clear temporary faults or error states.

CCR.Complaint: Battery not charging and low battery alarms displayed during transport.
CCR.Cause: Observed battery contacts clean and seated properly; ventilator self-test indicates charging fault; swapping battery module confirms persistent failure.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to Biomedical Engineering for repair.

helpfulDetails: Battery module swapped with known-good module
Final device status: Out of Service

title: Drager Evita Infinity V500 – O₂ Sensor Drift or Calibration Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting erratic O₂ readings, calibration failures, and sensor drift on Drager Evita Infinity V500 ventilators.

steps.title: Verify Power and Warm-Up
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator has been powered on for the manufacturer-recommended warm-up period. Sensors may report drift immediately after power-on; allowing proper warm-up can stabilize readings.

steps.title: Check Gas Supply Integrity
steps.instructions: Confirm wall O₂ and air pressures are within nominal range using the built-in pressure indicators or an independent manometer. Inspect for kinks, leaks, or faulty flow meters that could affect sensor readings.

steps.title: Inspect O₂ Sensor Module
steps.instructions: Confirm the sensor is correctly seated in its connector. Loose connections can cause intermittent drift errors. Look for visible contamination, corrosion, or moisture on sensor contacts.

steps.title: Run Calibration Procedure
steps.instructions: Perform a standard calibration using the ventilator menu or calibration kit. Note whether the device completes calibration successfully or fails at a specific step. A failed calibration suggests the sensor may be outside its usable lifespan or defective.

steps.title: Swap With Known Good Sensor (If Available)
steps.instructions: Replace the suspect O₂ sensor with a verified, functional sensor. Re-run calibration and verify that readings stabilize. If replacement resolves the issue, the original sensor is likely at fault.

steps.title: Check Software and Firmware
steps.instructions: Ensure the ventilator is running the latest approved firmware version. Some sensor drift errors can be triggered by outdated software; updating may resolve false drift alarms.

steps.title: Document Observations
steps.instructions: Note all abnormal readings, calibration failure steps, alarms, and any corrective actions taken.

CCR.Complaint: Ventilator displayed O₂ sensor drift and failed calibration during pre-use check.
CCR.Cause: Sensor module failed calibration; readings remained unstable despite proper warm-up and correct gas supply.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced sensor with verified module and successfully completed calibration. Device returned to service. Original sensor labeled for service/bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Sensor warm-up time and ventilator uptime

title: Drager Evita Infinity V500 – Display Unit or Control Panel Communication Errors
description: Guide to troubleshoot communication errors between the display unit/control panel and main system on Drager Evita Infinity V500 ventilators, focusing on external causes first.

steps.title: Ensure Proper Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Verify the ventilator is plugged into a working wall outlet. Check battery charge level; a low battery can sometimes trigger communication warnings. Confirm the device powers on normally. Why: Power instability can mimic control panel faults.

steps.title: Inspect Physical Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm the display unit cable (if removable) is fully seated at both ends. Check for bent pins, loose connectors, or visible damage. Why: Loose or damaged connectors are a common external cause of communication errors.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Interference
steps.instructions: Ensure no strong electromagnetic interference (EMI) sources are near the ventilator (large motors, cordless phones, wireless routers). Move the device slightly and observe if errors persist. Why: EMI can temporarily disrupt communication between modules.

steps.title: Restart the Device Safely
steps.instructions: Perform a controlled shutdown following the ventilator’s standard procedure. Wait ~30 seconds, then power it back on. Observe if the display unit initializes correctly and errors clear. Why: Some communication faults are temporary and reset after power cycling.

steps.title: Verify Software Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the device firmware is up to date (per hospital policy). Look for any logs or error codes related to display module communication. Why: Firmware inconsistencies can cause intermittent communication failures.

steps.title: Swap Display Unit (If Available)
steps.instructions: If a spare display unit is available, connect it to the ventilator. Confirm if errors persist. Why: Helps isolate whether the fault is in the display or main system.

CCR.Complaint: Display panel shows communication error and is unresponsive.
CCR.Cause: Observed that display cable was seated but errors persisted after power cycling; no visible external damage; errors repeated intermittently.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to Biomedical Engineering / vendor for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Fails Automatic Self-Test
description: Troubleshooting guide for Drager Apollo anesthesia machine failing automatic power-on self-test and not entering ready mode.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is not connected to an active patient. If failure occurs during room setup, ensure no induction has begun. If a patient is already present, move them to another verified anesthesia machine immediately. Self-test failures indicate safety-critical issues. Do not attempt to bypass startup checks.

steps.title: Confirm AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is plugged directly into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Check that the outlet is live (use outlet tester if available). Ensure the power cord is fully seated at both the wall and the rear of the machine. Low or unstable input power can cause incomplete self-tests or false failure messages.

steps.title: Verify Gas Supply Pressures
steps.instructions: Confirm O₂, Air, and N₂O pipeline hoses are securely connected. Check wall source pressure gauges for proper operating range. Inspect backup cylinders (if installed) for adequate pressure and open valves. The Apollo performs gas system integrity checks during startup. Low or absent supply pressure will cause test failure.

steps.title: Check Vaporizers
steps.instructions: Ensure vaporizers are properly seated and locked. Confirm no vaporizer is between detents (partially engaged). Turn all vaporizers OFF before initiating self-test. Improperly seated vaporizers can trigger leak or configuration errors.

steps.title: Inspect Breathing Circuit Assembly
steps.instructions: Confirm circuit is assembled correctly. Inspiratory and expiratory limbs are connected. APL valve is not fully closed. CO₂ absorber canister is properly seated and locked. Inspect for visible cracks or missing seals. Leak tests are part of the automatic self-test. An improperly assembled circuit will cause failure.

steps.title: Check Water Trap and Flow Sensors
steps.instructions: Inspect the water trap for condensation buildup. Drain or replace if full. Ensure it is properly seated. Moisture accumulation can interfere with flow measurement and sensor calibration checks.

steps.title: Restart the System
steps.instructions: Power down completely. Wait 30 seconds. Power back on and allow full self-test cycle to complete without interruption. Observe exact error message, any unusual sounds, and whether failure occurs at a consistent stage. Document the precise failure screen.

steps.title: Remove Optional Accessories
steps.instructions: If equipped, disconnect auxiliary monitors and remove external modules not required for operation. Retry the self-test. Peripheral communication errors can cause startup faults.

CCR.Complaint: Anesthesia machine failed automatic self-test during morning setup. Will not enter ready mode.
CCR.Cause: Self-test failure traced to improperly seated vaporizer causing leak test error.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated and locked vaporizer properly. Restarted system. Self-test completed successfully. Device returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (Returned to Service or Sent to Repair)

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine – Ventilator Drive Gas Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for Ventilator Drive Gas Failure alarms on the Drager Apollo anesthesia machine, focusing on external gas supply and safe escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being ventilated by an alternate method (manual bag or backup ventilator). Do not troubleshoot while the machine is actively supporting a patient unless absolutely necessary and safe. Ventilator drive gas failure directly affects mechanical ventilation and is a patient safety priority.

steps.title: Verify Medical Air Supply to the Machine
steps.instructions: The Apollo ventilator drive system relies on medical air. Check that the medical air hose is connected securely at both the wall outlet and rear of the machine. Verify the wall air outlet is functioning by testing with another device if available. Inspect for visible kinks or damage to the hose. Loss of medical air supply is the most common external cause of a drive gas failure alarm.

steps.title: Confirm Pipeline Pressure
steps.instructions: Observe the pipeline pressure gauges on the machine. Confirm medical air pressure is within normal range (typically ~50 psi / 3.5–5 bar). Check whether the machine is displaying a low air pipeline pressure alarm in addition to drive gas failure. If pressure is low or absent, verify the zone valve is open, confirm no construction or facility gas interruption, and contact Facilities if a supply issue is suspected.

steps.title: Check for Air Cylinder Backup (If Equipped)
steps.instructions: Confirm a cylinder is installed. Check cylinder pressure. Ensure the cylinder valve is open. If pipeline air fails and no cylinder backup is available, the ventilator cannot generate drive pressure.

steps.title: Inspect for Simultaneous O₂ Issues
steps.instructions: Determine whether only air pressure is affected or both air and oxygen pressures are abnormal. If both gases are abnormal, this may indicate a broader supply or internal manifold issue rather than isolated drive gas failure.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Machine (After Patient Is Safe)
steps.instructions: Turn the machine OFF. Wait 30 seconds. Restart and observe the startup check. This can clear transient pneumatic control errors. If the alarm clears and pipeline pressures are normal, monitor closely.

steps.title: Listen for Abnormal Internal Sounds
steps.instructions: After restart, listen for abnormal hissing, continuous venting, or failure to pressurize during self-test. Persistent abnormal sounds may indicate internal regulator failure, pneumatic valve malfunction, or internal leak. Do not open the unit or attempt internal pneumatic repair.

CCR.Complaint: Anesthesia reported ‘Ventilator Drive Gas Failure’ alarm during case setup. Ventilator would not initiate.
CCR.Cause: Medical air pipeline disconnected at rear of machine. No air pressure detected on gauge.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected medical air hose and verified normal pipeline pressure. Restarted machine; ventilator passed self-test.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (Returned to service or Sent to repair)

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Fresh Gas Decoupling Valve Faults
description: Guide for troubleshooting fresh gas decoupling valve faults on the Drager Apollo anesthesia machine, emphasizing external checks before internal repair.

steps.title: Ensure the Patient Is Safely Supported
steps.instructions: If the machine is in use, immediately transition the patient to manual ventilation or an alternate anesthesia machine. Do not troubleshoot on an actively ventilated patient unless absolutely necessary and safe. Patient safety is the priority.

steps.title: Confirm Proper System Startup
steps.instructions: Power cycle the machine if appropriate. Allow the full automatic self-test to complete. Note whether the fault appears during startup or only when entering ventilation mode. If the fault appears during self-test, this may indicate internal valve or pneumatic control issues.

steps.title: Inspect the CO₂ Absorber Canister
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the CO₂ absorber canister. Ensure it is locked fully into place. Inspect seals and O-rings for cracks, flattening, or debris. Confirm no absorbent granules are caught in the sealing surfaces. Improper seating can cause pressure and flow irregularities that trigger decoupling valve faults.

steps.title: Check the Breathing Circuit Assembly
steps.instructions: Verify inspiratory and expiratory limbs are correctly connected. Inspect for kinks, occlusions, or excessive moisture. Ensure the APL valve is not improperly set during testing. Confirm no accessories (e.g., bacterial filters) are occluded. Flow restriction or abnormal pressure feedback may cause the decoupling system to fault.

steps.title: Inspect Fresh Gas Flow Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm appropriate fresh gas flow rates. Avoid extremely low flows during initial testing. Observe whether the fault occurs at specific flow settings. Improper flow configuration can mimic decoupling valve malfunction.

steps.title: Perform a Leak Test
steps.instructions: Run a full system leak test. Observe for abnormal leak values. Pay attention to pressure stability during the test. A significant system leak may prevent proper decoupling valve operation.

steps.title: Listen for Abnormal Sounds
steps.instructions: During ventilation: listen for clicking irregularities. Note delayed or absent mechanical actuation sounds. Observe inconsistent tidal volume delivery. Abnormal mechanical response may suggest internal pneumatic or valve actuator issues.

steps.title: Inspect for Moisture or Contamination
steps.instructions: Check water traps and circuit components. Inspect for condensation entering internal pathways. Verify scavenging connections are not obstructed. Excess moisture can interfere with pneumatic valve operation.

steps.title: Review Error Logs (If Available)
steps.instructions: Access system logs through the service menu (without performing internal service functions). Note recurring valve or pressure sensor errors. Document specific fault codes for repair escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Anesthesia reported “Fresh Gas Decoupling Valve Fault” alarm during mechanical ventilation. Ventilator would not deliver consistent tidal volumes.
CCR.Cause: CO₂ absorber canister found improperly seated, causing system pressure instability during leak test.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated absorber canister, replaced worn O-ring, completed leak test successfully. Ventilator function restored. Machine returned to service.

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Flow Sensor Calibration Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting flow sensor calibration errors on Drager Apollo anesthesia machines, emphasizing external checks before assuming sensor or module failure.

steps.title: Confirm Machine Power and Startup
steps.instructions: Ensure the Apollo is plugged into a verified AC power source or fully charged battery. Proper startup is required for calibration routines; unstable power can trigger false flow sensor errors.

steps.title: Check Gas Supply and Pressures
steps.instructions: Verify O₂, N₂O, and Air pressures are within manufacturer-specified ranges (typically 200–600 kPa). Low or fluctuating pressures can prevent proper flow sensor calibration. Inspect gas hoses for kinks or loose connections.

steps.title: Inspect Flow Sensor Pathway
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the pediatric and adult flow sensor connectors. Remove any moisture, condensation, or foreign matter in the sensor housing or breathing circuit. Ensure sensors are seated properly; a misaligned or partially disconnected sensor can fail calibration.

steps.title: Check Breathing Circuit Configuration
steps.instructions: Make sure the correct circuit type is selected in the machine settings. Disconnect or bypass any added adapters that could interfere with sensor readings.

steps.title: Run Automatic Calibration Again
steps.instructions: Initiate the flow sensor calibration routine per the on-screen prompts. Observe for any error codes or specific messages; note if calibration fails consistently for one sensor only.

steps.title: Swap Sensors if Possible
steps.instructions: If multiple flow sensors are available, swap the suspected sensor with a known good sensor. If the error follows the sensor, the sensor itself may be defective. If the error stays on the same port, the issue may be internal to the machine.

steps.title: Inspect Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Excessive humidity, condensation, or cold gas lines can impact sensor calibration. Allow the machine and gases to reach room temperature if recently moved.

CCR.Complaint: Flow sensor calibration fails during startup; machine shows error FS-03.
CCR.Cause: Observed condensation in the flow sensor and loose sensor connector. Calibration failed on both adult and pediatric sensors.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared condensation, reseated sensor connectors, re-ran calibration successfully. Machine passed self-test and ready for use.

helpfulDetails: Final machine status (Ready / Out of Service)

title: Drager Apollo Anesthesia Machine - Touchscreen or Control Knob Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting unresponsive touchscreen or control knobs on the Apollo anesthesia machine, focusing on external checks before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Confirm AC Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is connected to a reliable AC outlet. Check the battery charge level; a low battery can cause UI response issues. Why: Ensures the device has stable power; erratic touch or knob behavior can occur with insufficient power.

steps.title: Inspect Touchscreen and Knobs for Physical Obstructions
steps.instructions: Check for protective films, debris, or liquids on the touchscreen surface. Rotate each control knob fully to ensure it moves freely without sticking. Why: Physical interference is a common, easily fixable cause of unresponsiveness.

steps.title: Reboot the Machine
steps.instructions: Perform a proper shutdown and power-on cycle following Apollo startup procedure. Observe if touchscreen and knobs respond during POST (Power-On Self-Test). Why: Software glitches can temporarily disable user interface inputs.

steps.title: Check for External Device Interference
steps.instructions: Remove USB drives, external monitors, or accessory modules connected to the Apollo. Why: Some connected peripherals can cause touchscreen or control knob conflicts.

steps.title: Verify Software Version and Recent Updates
steps.instructions: Check the software version displayed on startup. Note any recent software updates that may correlate with the failure. Why: Certain firmware issues may affect UI responsiveness and may require manufacturer intervention.

steps.title: Calibrate Touchscreen (If Available in Menu)
steps.instructions: Navigate to the calibration option (if responsive). Follow on-screen instructions to recalibrate. Why: Miscalibrated touchscreens can appear unresponsive even when functional.

steps.title: Observe Behavior with Minimal Configuration
steps.instructions: Disconnect nonessential modules (e.g., monitoring interfaces) and restart. Test touchscreen and knobs. Why: Confirms whether the failure is isolated or caused by a module/software conflict.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen and control knobs are unresponsive during anesthesia setup.
CCR.Cause: After verifying power, removing obstructions, and rebooting, touchscreen and knobs remained unresponsive.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for manufacturer repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: AC outlet tested and confirmed stable
Observed any screen flicker or partial responsiveness
Noted control knob stiffness or lack of detent feedback
Device final status before removal from service
